channel.title: Climate Risk (Google Alerts)
channel.description: Últimos artigos de Climate Risk (Google Alerts)

item.title: Alumni Spotlight: Tamara Coger '16 MPA | Watson School of International and Public Affairs
item.description:
Tamara Coger, a 2016 graduate of Brown’s Master of Public Affairs (MPA) program, works as the policy director of the Global Restoration Initiative at the World Resources Institute, where she has spent the past eight years helping shape international approaches to climate resilience, ecosystem restoration and locally led adaptation. Her work spans forests, finance and resilience of frontline communities. It is technical, global, and urgent.

And beneath the policy frameworks and strategy documents is something else: a deep love of nature, a commitment to climate justice, a belief in progress for good, and a conviction that solutions are within reach — values which were sharpened during her time at Brown.

At Watson, Coger found intellectual flexibility and training that crossed traditional academic boundaries, which enabled her to turn her environmental concern into a career.

Before attending Brown, she studied at American University, where she majored in international relations and affairs. After completing her undergraduate degree, she worked in Washington, D.C., in international development. She joined USAID-funded projects and worked across sectors, including health and agriculture. It was valuable early-career experience that, over time, paved the way to a clearer calling.

“As I was exploring different areas, I realized that I really wanted to focus on climate change and environmental spheres,” said Coger. To make that pivot, she turned to graduate schools that could provide the policy grounding needed to transition into climate work.

What first attracted her to Brown was practicality. Watson’s accelerated one-year structure meant she could deepen her expertise without stepping away from her career for two full years. Just as important was the program’s flexibility. Though not branded as an environmental policy degree, Watson offered something Coger valued more: the freedom to shape the curriculum around her own goals.

“It was something that I could tailor to align with my interests,” she said.

At Brown, some of the most influential classes were not what she expected. A course on environmental impact assessment sounded, at first glance, highly procedural — a class about learning how to conduct technical reviews. Instead, it centered on justice, examining how environmental assessments could be used to promote fairer outcomes, especially for communities that bear disproportionate burdens from pollution, climate disruption and underinvestment.

“It was very different than what I expected,” she said, “and way more useful.”

This course and others like it became foundational to how she thinks about policy design today, and foreshadowed the direction of her later work.

Beyond explicitly climate-focused coursework, Brown provided Coger proficiency in adjacent disciplines. Economics classes, statistics and Geographic Information Systems (GIS) training expanded her toolkit and taught her how to communicate across specialties.

“I know I’m never going to be an economist or a GIS expert, but having that foundation and being conversant in those topics has been really useful.”

Through her MPA project and a faculty connection, she secured an opportunity at the Organization for Economic Co-operation and Development (OECD) in Paris, where she supported research on synergies and trade-offs between climate adaptation and mitigation policies in agriculture. It was exactly the kind of work she hoped to do.

She described the experience as unique and influential, as she saw how policy recommendations develop, learned inside a multilateral institution, and worked alongside representatives from countries across the OECD.

“It was an opportunity I have always been grateful for,” she said.

The experience remains meaningful years later. She is still in touch with her former supervisor and has crossed paths with the organization multiple times since. Equally important were the friendships she formed at Brown. Because the Watson program moves at an intense pace, students bond quickly.

“It’s sort of a bonding experience to go through that together,” she said. “It’s a close cohort, and you’re doing this intensive program together; the structure is conducive to fast friendships.”

After Brown, Coger joined the World Resources Institute. She began on the monitoring, evaluation and learning team, working across the organization on strategic planning and program assessments. In many ways, it was a direct application of skills developed at Brown. She analyzed impact, synthesized information and helped teams improve outcomes. From there, she moved into climate resilience and adaptation.

One major focus was shifting international climate policy conversations to elevate adaptation funding alongside mitigation. For years, much of the climate agenda centered on reducing emissions — essential work, but only part of the equation. Communities are already experiencing climate disruption. They also need resources to adapt. That led to one of the initiatives that Coger is most proud of: locally led adaptation.

The concept is straightforward and powerful. Communities closest to climate impacts should have meaningful authority over decisions affecting their future, along with direct access to funding and support.

“It’s easy to agree with in theory,” she said. “But what does that actually look like in practice?”

Her work helped answer that question through frameworks, principles and examples for governments and institutions. The approach gained traction internationally, with endorsements from major organizations and references in the Biden administration’s climate policy. Although she is careful not to overstate policy wins, she believes changing narratives matters as “it helps shift toward a more bottom-up and localized approach.”

Today, as policy director of WRI’s Global Restoration Initiative, she focuses on ecosystems — particularly forests — and leads policy efforts tied to restoration.

item.description: less rainfall in winter and spring, particularly in the eastern half of Australia; higher daytime temperatures in southern areas; increased frost risk ...

item.title: Evading Notice and Comment in the Second Trump Administration
item.description:
In the past year, the Trump administration has curtailed public participation in agency rulemaking in broad and novel ways. The administration has used tactics that involve all three branches of government, and many of these efforts involve environmental regulatory issues. This paper evaluates some of those strategies, analyzing how the administration has diverged from historical practice as it seeks to achieve quick deregulatory results with limited process or expertise, data, and feedback from stakeholders.

Our analysis explains how the administration has restricted public participation by: (1) using statutory exceptions, (2) asking courts to intervene, and (3) collaborating with Congress; and identifies legal risks those tactics raise. It also evaluates some of those strategies, analyzing how the administration has diverged from historical practice as it seeks to achieve quick deregulatory results with limited process or expertise, data, and feedback from stakeholders.

These approaches share a common feature — many take immediate legal effect, achieving deregulatory outcomes before courts can review them. Even if courts ultimately find that the process is unlawful, the administration’s actions may have already caused on-the-ground effects, including, for example, extending compliance deadlines that benefit some regulated industries or undermining regulatory certainty that is necessary for projects to continue.
item.dc:creator: Autor não encontrado

item.title: Connecting Ambition and Capital at the 2026 Blue Economy and Finance Forum
item.description:
• Connecting Ambition and Capital at the 2026 Blue Economy and Finance Forum

    June 16, 2026 ByCarolyn Gruber

    “Only one thing now stands between the ocean and the capital it needs: the decision to act. And a delayed decision is never neutral. The cost of waiting…is paid by those who come after us,” said HSH Prince Albert II of Monaco at the Blue Economy and Finance Forum in Monaco last month.

    This year’s BEFF brought together policymakers, business leaders, investors, banks, pension funds, sovereign wealth funds, and philanthropies to strengthen the science, policy, and finance connections at the core of the sustainable blue economy. Over the two days, a few key themes emerged.

    1. Ocean (and climate) risk is financial risk.

    And that risk is visible in very real balance sheets. People, critical infrastructure, utilities, coastal real estate, defense and military assets, oil and gas, fisheries, shipping routes, and global supply chains are increasingly exposed, with US

    $8.4 trillionin assets and revenues are at risk in the next 15 years. Physical damage costs are rising, and system-wide instability is growing.

    2. The blue economy is an investable asset class…almost.

    The ocean economy fuels global growth. Last year,

    J.P. Morgan estimatedthe value of ocean-related activity — from shipping and fisheries to tourism and energy — at $2.5 trillion annually. Ocean-focused initiatives areattracting capitalfrom investors seeking both impact and profit. Despite this momentum, investments in the blue economy continue to face structural hurdles that make them less competitive.

    Take the

    cost of capital, for example. Because we are measuring returns in dollars, the cost of capital in the blue economy — the minimum returns a company must earn to justify putting capital at risk — is still too high. This is because the contexts where these investments need to flow (i.e., emerging or developing economies) have extra risks to cover, whether foreign exchange risk, inflation risk, or governance risk.

    During the BEFF, Tom Hall, the Global Head of

    Social Impact and Philanthropy at UBS, framed two ways to reduce the cost of capital: (1) boost returns, or (2) lower risk. Boosting returns can be done but is rare (i.e., a unicorn), but lowering risk is very much within our grasp. Within the fiduciary constraints of how a bank typically operates, Hall says, “We need to create investment opportunities that both meet market risk-adjusted return and bring a large supply of capital at a sustainable price in a way that can be invested across a themed portfolio. This can drive deeper, more meaningful impact.” Impact funds, made up of multiple asset classes, geographies, and themes (e.g., climate, ocean, or health), can capture the value of the unicorns and, essentially, subsize the cost of capital for highly impactful projects that would not otherwise be funded. Impact investing can drive economic prosperity and solve problems at the same time.

    3. Flexible, patient capital can scale the blue economy.

    Blended finance mechanisms have been successfully deployed at scale in development contexts but are still emerging in the blue finance space. The magic of blended finance lies in its flexibility. A flexible capital stack lets investors participate in a project, program, or initiative based on their own appetite for risk and return.

    In the cap stack, there’s something for everyone! This is especially the case in ocean finance, which includes an extra foundational tranche of funding supported by grants or

    philanthropic capital. This funding serves as a true “first-loss” cushion that makes the entire stack investible for parties above it. Next, large development finance institutions (e.g., World Bank or IDB) and commercial banks take on senior debt — the safest position, first to receive repayment of their principal and interest, and carrying the lowest interest rates. In the middle, institutional lenders (e.g., Development Finance Institutions likeBritish International Investmentin the UK andProparcoin France) and private equity groups assume mezzanine debt, accepting higher interest rates in exchange for higher risk. At the top of the stack, partners and sponsors likeOcean 14 CapitalorAqua-Sparkabsorb common equity — the last to receive a return and the first to absorb losses, but with the greatest upside if the project succeeds. The right mix of investors — each calibrated to a different risk-return profile — distributes risk across the stack.

    A flexible stack allows the World Bank to sit comfortably alongside a philanthropy like the David and Lucille Packard Foundation and a private equity fund like Ocean 14 Capital — each achieving its own goals while collectively financing a project or program that none would fund alone. Reflecting on the need for flexibility, Minna Epps, Global Ocean Policy Director at the IUCN Centre for Policy and Law, emphasized the difference between the economic viability of a coastal resilience project and the invest-ability of that project, saying “Between now and the next BEFF, we should focus on unlocking more patient, flexible, and accessible finance for the countries and communities that are most dependent on a healthy ocean, while scaling practical mechanisms such as the

    Blue Natural Capital Financing Facilitythat help turn promising ocean conservation and restoration initiatives into investable, sustainable projects.”

    4. Governance still matters.

    The blue economy offers coastal communities and Large Ocean States a genuine pathway to sustainable and resilient development. But, a truly regenerative and invest-able blue economy requires policy coherence across sectors and scales, and strong local institutions and transparent governance structures that give coastal communities a meaningful voice in the decisions that shape their futures.

    Governance was the focus of a Stimson Center side event at the BEFF, which showcased policy incentives, new ways to pool and disburse funding, insurance mechanisms, and creative public-private partnerships that have been effective in mobilizing capital in coastal communities.

    Several concrete examples illustrate what this looks like in practice. The IUCN’s

    Blue Carbon Accelerator Fundis driving financial flows at scale through policy engagement. The Government of Seychelles, which issued the world’sfirst sovereign blue bond, is reorienting its tourism and fisheries industries toward higher value, more resilient models that can protect existing jobs while creating new ones. The Commonwealth Secretariat, in partnership with the Prince Albert II of Monaco Foundation, is working to increase investment in ocean conservation in Large Ocean States through itsIncubator, and by providing countries with guides toBlue Bond IssuanceandOpportunities in Sustainable Blue Financeto help raise ocean conservation finance.NOAH ReGen Planet’s BLUE+ IMPACT Marketplaceaims to translate multiple forms of environmental value, including carbon, biodiversity, pollution reduction, ecosystem restoration, and social impact, into credible, measurable, and financeable instruments that can generate long-term revenue streams for countries while supporting reinvestment into national priorities and local communities.Rareissued a first-of-its-kindSmall-Scale Fisheries Impact Bondto finance community-led, government-backed co-management of small-scale fisheries. ORRAA is using theSea Change Impact Financing Facility (SCIFF)to share practical pathways for countries seeking access to capital markets for ocean investment. TheReOcean Fundis working to channel €100 million towards transforming blue foods, protecting ocean ecosystems, and reducing marine pollution.

    The Stimson Center’s

    Climate and Ocean Risk Vulnerability Initiativetakes an integrated approach to assessing and reducing coastal risks and asserts that the blue economy only delivers long-term returns if the underlying natural capital is protected. Several of the most recent CORVI assessments inArubaandBelize Cityreference the key role thatnature-based solutionsplay in long-term coastal resilience. Sally Yozell, Senior Advisor to Stimson’s Environmental Security Program, said, “It’s reassuring to see that nature-based solutions are being integrated into the adaptation project pipeline, but we need to move more expeditiously and scale up the work more quickly.”

    5. Blue finance is moving in the right direction, and the BEFF is helping.

    The broader mood at this year’s BEFF reflected a field that is maturing. The growing diversity of actors — spanning finance, policy, science, and civil society — points to an expanding ecosystem of institutions that see

    regenerative economies(blue and otherwise) as both a necessity and an opportunity. Frédéric Degret, Founder and CEO of NOAH ReGen, said, “In the leadup to the next BEFF, we need practical demonstration platforms: country-level structures where natural capital, blue and green carbon, biodiversity, nature-positive infrastructure, and blended finance can be translated into institutional-grade investment pipelines. Regenerative economies are within reach, but require early and deliberate integration of finance, science, governance, and community participation into their design.”

    Dr. Daniel Wilde, Head of Oceans for the Commonwealth Secretariat, noted that “achieving international ocean conservation goals requires a big increase in investment in Large Ocean States, and BEFF is an important forum for advancing that goal.”

    The question of scale was a recurring theme. Participants were broadly aligned that advancing a regenerative blue economy requires more than capital alone. It requires stronger coordination across governance levels, clearer and measurable standards, and a shared commitment to aligning investment with long-term impact. Minna Epps said, “The blue economy is no longer a niche concept—it is becoming a central pillar of sustainable development. The next step is to shift from ambition to implementation by directing capital toward regenerative approaches that restore ocean health while generating livelihoods and resilience.”

    HSH Prince Albert II of Monaco framed the underlying stakes: the ocean, he said, should be understood “not as a risk to be managed, but as the defining opportunity of our time.”

    Seas the day, indeed.

    Carolyn Gruber is a Fellow and Deputy Director of the Environmental Security Program at the Stimson Center.


    Sources: UN Environment Programme Finance Initiative; JP Morgan; Time; Investopedia; UBS; World Economic Forum; British International Investment; Proparco; Ocean 14 Capital; Aqua-Spark; IUCN Blue Natural Capital; World Bank; The Commonwealth; NOAH ReGen; Rare; Ocean Risk Alliance (ORRAA); ReOcean Fund; Stimson Center; World Resources Institute; Impact Investor
    Photo Credits: Courtesy of Philippe Fitte.
item.dc:creator: Autor não encontrado

item.title: London Climate Action Week - the role of finance
item.description:
London Climate Action Week - the role of finance

Bush House, Strand Campus, London

King’s Business School is marking the start of London Climate Action Week with two events on the role of finance in managing and adapting to climate change.

UNEP Transition Insurance Series: Insuring the transition and the climate-nature intersection

22 June, Bush House and online

08:30 – 13:00 BST

King's is pleased to host the UNEP Transition Insurance Series on 22 June 2026 at Bush House, London, ahead of the UNEP FI Global Roundtable. #GRT2026

Dr Ylva Baeckström, Senior Lecturer in Finance and Theme Lead (Sustainable Finance), Centre for Sustainable Business will open the series alongside Butch Bacani, UNEP Head of Insurance, setting the scene for discussions on the future of insurance in a rapidly changing risk landscape.

Two dedicated sessions will cover:

Emerging approaches to insurer transition planning across underwriting and investment activities, alongside strategies to address protection gaps and growing insurability challenges.

A pioneering methodology developed through the Principles for Sustainable Insurance (PSI) Working Group for Nature that translates ecosystem conditions into insurance-relevant risk signals for underwriting and risk models.

Programme:

8:30 Light breakfast and registration

9:00 UNEP Antitrust Statement and introductory remarks

  • Emily Farrimond, Partner, ESG & Sustainability Lead, Baringa
  • Ylva Baeckstrom, Senior lecturer in Insurance at King’s Business School
  • Butch Bacani, Head of Insurance, United Nations Environment Programme (UNEP)
  • Seth Eshun, Project Coordinator, Transition Insurance, United Nations Environment Programme (UNEP)

10:15 Session 1: Insuring the transition

  • Leah Ramatour, Director of Environmental Sustainability, Aviva
  • Peter Tufano, Baker Foundation Professor at Harvard Business School
  • Fiona Watson, Vice President, World Business Council for Sustainable Development

10:45 Break

11:00 Session 2: Insurance at the Climate-Nature Intersection

  • David Maslo, CEO, ARC Ltd.
  • Babs Dijkshoorn, Director Sustainability at Achmea
  • Thomas Viegas, Group Nature Lead, Aviva
  • Nicola Ranger, Executive Director, Earth Capital Nexus and Professor, London School of Economics
  • Bjorn Alfthan, Senior Sustainable Business Executive, Gard
  • Swenja Sturminski, Managing Director for Climate & Sustainability, Marsh
  • Dave Jones, Director of the Climate Risk Initiative, UC Berkeley School of Law

12:15 Lunch

Register your interest here - in-person places may be limited.

The Resilience Shift: Mobilising Capital for Adaptation

Monday 22 June, 5:00-7:00pm

A panel session co-hosted with Baringa focused on mobilising capital for adaptation and resilience. It brings together leaders from academia, finance, and policy to explore how physical climate risks are being integrated into decision-making, how private capital can be unlocked, and what market, policy, and governance shifts are needed to scale resilience investments.

Register your interest in attending here: Baringa London Climate Week 2026 - Event Registration

Co-Chaired by Dr Ylva Baeckström, Senior Lecturer in Finance and Theme Lead (Sustainable Finance), Centre for Sustainable Business with Emily Farrimond, Partner Climate & Sustainability at Baringa.

Panellists: Dr Katie Manning, Lecturer in Climate Change, Business, and Society at King’s College; Dazzle Bhujwala, Senior Director Investor Network at Ceres; Joe Robinson, Director Investment Stewardship & Sustainability at Equitix; Cathy Ansell, Executive Director Climate, Nature & Social at JPMorgan Chase.

Search for another event
item.dc:creator: Autor não encontrado

item.title: Climate Risk and the Future of Northeast Agriculture - Morning Ag Clips
item.description:
BOSTON — Today [June 16, 2026], Farm Aid and the Northeast Climate Disaster Relief Network (NCDRN) released “When Disaster Becomes the Norm: Climate Risk and the Future of Northeast Agriculture,” a new white paper that explores the growing toll of climate-driven events on the region’s agricultural economy and communities.

According to the report, the U.S. now averages 19 disasters costing more than $1 billion each year, up from just three per year in the 1980s. In the Northeast, record floods, heatwaves and rainfall are causing mounting losses, while farmers lack reliable federal aid, timely state support to respond to these pressures and access to crop insurance.

The white paper highlights how these climate disasters are creating compounding impacts across the food system, including crop losses, land and labor loss, supply chain disruption and an ongoing farmer mental health crisis.

Among the report’s findings:

  • According to the latest National Climate Assessment, the Northeast is already warmer and wetter than a century ago, with average temperatures over 2°F higher than in the early 1900s and a significant rise in extreme precipitation events, which are unusually heavy bursts of rain or snow.
  • The National Oceanic and Atmospheric Administration’s database, while authoritative for large-scale disasters, excludes regional floods, hailstorms and frosts that cause hundreds of millions in damage.
  • From 2001 to 2022, total indemnity payments for the five primary weather-related causes (drought, excess rain, hail, heat and freeze) reached $118.7 billion nationwide.
  • Climate disasters disproportionately impact BIPOC farmers, farmworkers and other historically underserved communities.

“Farmers in the Northeast struggle with barriers to crop insurance, delayed and inaccessible federal aid and increasingly unpredictable weather,” said Farm Aid Policy and Advocacy Manager Hank Tremblay. “Elected officials, funders and nonprofit agriculture organizations must work together to create a comprehensive safety net that meets the needs of all our region’s farmers.”

The report outlines a roadmap for building a stronger agricultural disaster response system, including establishing permanent state-level disaster relief funds, reducing barriers to assistance, investing in climate resilience and ensuring that farmers and farmworkers help shape the programs intended to support them.

For more information, find the full report here: farmaid.org/ncdrn-whitepaper.

Farm Aid’s mission is to build a vibrant, family farm-centered system of agriculture in America. Farm Aid artists and board members Willie Nelson, Neil Young, John Mellencamp, Dave Matthews, Margo Price and Nathaniel Rateliff host an annual festival to raise funds to support Farm Aid’s work with family farmers and to inspire people to choose family farm food. Since 1985, Farm Aid, with the support of the artists who contribute their performances each year, has raised more than $90 million to support programs that help farmers thrive, expand the reach of the Good Food Movement, take action to change the dominant system of industrial agriculture and promote food from family farms.

NCDRN is a coalition of agricultural service providers, advocates and farmers working to ensure that farmers and workers in the Northeast are equitably and effectively resourced before, during and after climate disasters. Developed by a coalition of agricultural service providers, advocates and farmers across the region, the report details how climate disasters are reshaping agriculture in the Northeastern United States faster than existing support systems can adapt.

—Farm Aid
item.dc:creator: Autor não encontrado

item.title: Nearly all children globally exposed to at least one climate hazard: Report
item.description:
Nearly all children globally exposed to at least one climate hazard: Report

Report highlights the growing threats posed by climate change and calls for the green transition to be accelerated.

Almost all children across the globe are exposed to at least one climate hazard and the situation is expected to worsen unless greenhouse gas emissions are urgently reduced, says a report by UNICEF.

The report, published on Tuesday, warns that climate hazards pose a threat to children on multiple fronts, with nearly half of the world’s children exposed to at least three such hazards, putting their health, education and survival at risk.

Recommended Stories

list of 3 items
  • list 1 of 3Trump admin’s cancellation of wind energy projects causes business turmoil
  • list 2 of 3Zimbabwe’s climate migrants fear eviction as crackdown intensifies
  • list 3 of 3Australia warns El Nino weather pattern set to be strongest in decades

“The lives of children continue to be upended by the impact of heatwaves, wildfires, droughts, and floods,” said UNICEF Executive Director Catherine Russell. “Half of the world’s children are now living with at least three overlapping climate threats shaping their daily lives.”

The report highlights the growing threats posed by climate change and calls on governments and business leaders to accelerate the transition to renewable energy.

According to UNICEF’s report, 1.8 billion children are currently at risk from drought, while 1.2 billion are exposed to extreme heat, as warmer temperatures wreak havoc on the world’s water cycle.

Countries across Western Europe experienced a record-breaking heatwave last month, reaching temperatures not typically expected until the summer.

UNICEF also says that nearly every child is exposed to air pollution, while one billion are exposed to malaria.

Scientists have repeatedly warned that global warming must be limited to 1.5C (2.7F) above pre-industrial levels to avoid the worst effects of climate change.

Nearly 200 countries signed the Paris Agreement, aiming to curb global warming to that 1.5C mark. The accord came into force in November 2016.

Since then, scientists have repeatedly warned that the target is unlikely to be met.

In January, the United States formally withdrew from the Paris Agreement for a second time, following an order by President Donald Trump.
